response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I don't spend much time on social media, maybe a few times each week when I have a moment between shifts at the restaurant or in the evening after dealing with the family. But even with limited exposure, I can tell you that fake content is everywhere, and frankly, it irritates me. People share things without thinking, spreading nonsense that confuses everyone. When you're my age and you've seen how things work in the real world, you develop a certain skepticism about what you read online. I'm not one of those people who believes everything they see just because it has a nice picture or a dramatic headline. My father had no formal education but he taught me to question things, and my mother with her master's degree always emphasized thinking critically. That combination stuck with me. When I see something suspicious on Facebook or wherever, I simply don't engage with it. I don't share it, I don't comment, I just scroll past. Why waste energy on garbage? Some people get worked up, arguing in the comments, but that's pointless. The best approach is to stick with sources you know. I trust established news outlets, not random pages with names I've never heard of. If something seems too outrageous or too perfect, it probably is. I tell my four kids the same thing when they're on their phones constantly. They roll their eyes at me, but I've learned that the internet is full of people trying to manipulate you for clicks or money or political reasons. Living in Naples, you see enough drama in real life without inventing more online. My Christian faith teaches me that truth matters, and spreading lies, even unknowingly, is harmful. So my strategy is simple: be skeptical, don't rush to share, verify with reliable sources if something seems important, and mostly just limit how much time you spend in that digital mess. Life is complicated enough without adding fake news to the mix. Focus on what's real, what's in front of you, your family, your work, your actual community.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 50-year-old married man working part-time as a waiter in Naples with four children. His low openness suggests he's traditional and skeptical of new ideas, preferring familiar approaches. His low agreeableness indicates he's direct, skeptical, and not overly concerned with pleasing others in his communication. His moderate conscientiousness suggests he takes a practical but not overly systematic approach. His depression and moderate neuroticism may contribute to a somewhat negative or weary outlook. He uses social media only a few times weekly, suggesting limited engagement. His upper secondary education and mixed parental educational background inform his practical wisdom. His Christian beliefs provide a moral framework about truth. His occupation as a waiter gives him a working-class, practical perspective. The tone should be direct, somewhat skeptical, practical, and slightly weary or irritated. He would focus on simple, common-sense strategies rather than technical solutions, drawing on life experience and family values.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, skeptical, practical, somewhat weary and irritated, grounded in traditional values and life experience
